key_insight: A microscopic constitutive model links nonaffine elasticity and structural relaxation to explain stress overshoot, yielding, steady flow, and polymer strain hardening in amorphous solids.
engineering_relevance: It provides a unified, physically grounded way to predict full stress--strain curves of metallic and polymer glasses across strain rates using a small parameter set.
method: Nonaffine elasticity captures strain-driven loss of mechanical connectivity and the onset of elastic instability.
Irreversible many-body relaxation with a stretched-exponential exponent governs viscous dissipation and flow.
Finite chain extensibility is added for polymer glasses to reproduce strain hardening at large deformation.
evidence_signal: The model is validated by quantitatively reproducing experimental stress--strain curves for metallic glasses, polycarbonate, PMMA, and epoxy resins over a broad range of strain rates.
watch_item: The framework depends on fitted physically meaningful parameters and an empirically determined relaxation exponent, so predictive accuracy may vary if those inputs are unavailable or outside the tested material classes.
paper_title: Microscopic constitutive theory of stress overshoot, yielding, and strain hardening in amorphous materials
